title: Acute Compartment Syndrome
chief_complaint: I fractured my tibia playing soccer 8 hours ago. My cast was put on, but now my pain is getting worse - it's unbearable, much worse than a broken bone should feel. The pain medication isn't touching it. My foot feels numb and I can't move my toes.

physical_exam.Right_Leg: Long leg cast in place. Severe pain on passive stretch of toes (pathognomonic - stretches ischemic muscles). Tense, swollen compartments on palpation. Foot pale, cool.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Decreased sensation in first web space (deep peroneal nerve - first sign). Weak dorsiflexion of ankle and great toe. Dorsalis pedis pulse weakly palpable (pulselessness is LATE sign - do NOT rely on pulses to rule out compartment syndrome).
physical_exam.Compartments: Anterior compartment: Tense, firm. Pain out of proportion to injury.
physical_exam.Labs: CK 2,500 (rhabdomyolysis beginning). Compartment pressures: Anterior 65mmHg, Lateral 55mmHg, Deep posterior 48mmHg. Delta pressure (diastolic - compartment): 95-65 = 30 (<30 is diagnostic).

correct_diagnosis: Acute Compartment Syndrome of Right Leg (Anterior and Lateral Compartments) from Tibial Fracture
key_learning_points: Compartment syndrome = increased pressure within closed osteofascial compartment → capillary compression → tissue ischemia → muscle and nerve necrosis. IRREVERSIBLE damage after 6-8 hours

Classic 6 Ps: Pain out of proportion (EARLIEST, most sensitive), Pain with passive stretch (PATHOGNOMONIC), Paresthesia (first nerve sign), Paralysis (late), Pallor, Pulselessness (VERY LATE - do NOT wait for this!)

Most common causes: Tibial shaft fractures, forearm fractures, crush injuries, tight casts/dressings, burns, reperfusion injury. Most common sites: Lower leg (anterior compartment most common), forearm (volar compartment)

Diagnosis: CLINICAL. Compartment pressure measurements if equivocal exam (obtunded patient, pediatric). Delta pressure = diastolic BP - compartment pressure. Delta <30mmHg = compartment syndrome. Absolute pressure >30mmHg also diagnostic

Treatment: EMERGENCY FASCIOTOMY. Remove cast/splint, split all dressings down to skin. 4-compartment fasciotomy of leg (2 incisions: anterolateral + posteromedial). Forearm: Volar fasciotomy (carpal tunnel release + mobile wad). Leave wounds open, delayed primary closure or skin graft in 5-7 days. Monitor for rhabdomyolysis (IV fluids, monitor CK, renal function)
